one_line_takeaway: DLL-IDS shows that pairing a LID-based adversarial-example detector with a rerouting to a transfer-robust Label-Spreading ML classifier can raise a DL-IDS's worst-case accuracy under CW attack from 17.9% to 71.7% on NSL-KDD with negligible clean-traffic cost, though only white-box, non-adaptive, feature-level attacks are tested.
key_finding: On NSL-KDD, the undefended DL IDS collapses under strong attack (CW at 10% perturbation drops accuracy to 17.9%, and to ~5.7% at 20%), whereas DLL-IDS restores accuracy to 71.7% under CW and stays above 70% across all four attacks, while clean-example accuracy barely changes (91.8% for DLL-IDS vs 92.1% baseline). On CICIDS2018 the gap is even larger: baseline accuracy under FGSM/BIM/DeepFool/CW is 32.0/22.5/18.5/17.1% versus DLL-IDS 95.0/88.5/71.5/63.7%, with clean accuracy 98.1% vs 98.5%. The LID AE detector beats the DB/Artifact baseline in nearly all cells (e.g. NSL-KDD CW-20% detection 83.4% LID vs 89.6% Artifact is one of the few exceptions, but LID leads broadly, e.g. clean 96.9% vs 82.1%), and detection accuracy rises with larger perturbation. Label-Spreading is the most AE-robust ML model, with 81.0% overall accuracy on transferred AEs vs KNN 72.9%, DTC 61.2%, SVM 57.1%, LGR 54.6%.
core_contribution: The paper proposes DLL-IDS, a three-module intrusion-detection framework that hardens a deep-learning IDS against adversarial-example (AE) attacks by fusing a DL-based IDS, a novel LID-based AE detector, and a robust ML-based IDS. Its own novel claims are: (1) it reframes the AE-defense problem for IDS by insisting the system must judge BOTH whether an input is an AE AND whether the underlying traffic is inherently malicious, arguing that prior work (e.g. MANDA) wrongly treats every detected AE as malicious, which lets an attacker flood a bypass-deployed IDS with benign-derived AEs to trigger mass false alarms; (2) it is the first to port the local intrinsic dimensionality (LID) concept from computer-vision AE detection into IDS AE detection, using hidden-layer activation LID values fit with an SVM classifier; (3) it exploits the empirically low attack-transferability between DL and traditional ML models to route detected AEs to a Label-Spreading (LS) semi-supervised model, and offers a Rayleigh-quotient / normalized-Laplacian argument for why LS resists AEs. When an input is flagged as an AE the LS ML-IDS makes the maliciousness call; otherwise the DL-IDS is trusted, minimizing ML resource use on clean traffic.
threat_model: The paper defines an explicit threat model (Section 3). The attacker's goal is to force the IDS to misclassify traffic in either direction: crafting AEs from malicious traffic to evade alarms, or crafting AEs from benign traffic to trigger mass false alarms and destroy IDS availability. Attacks are categorized by prior knowledge into white-box, gray-box, and black-box. The actual evaluation uses only white-box, feature-level attacks (FLA) that perturb input feature vectors: FGSM, BIM, DeepFool, and CW. Perturbations are constrained to within 20% of the original data and, for NSL-KDD, applied only to the 35 dynamic features (6 persistent features left untouched) to respect domain/network constraints; attacks are applied only to samples the DL model originally classified correctly. Out of scope by the authors' own statement: non-lp similarity constraints such as generative-model and packet-obfuscation attacks, and adaptive attacks specifically targeting the combined detector pipeline.
limitations: By the authors' own scoping: (1) only white-box feature-level lp-bounded attacks are evaluated; generative-model and packet-obfuscation attacks and true black/gray-box settings are discussed but not tested. (2) No adaptive attacker is evaluated against the full three-module pipeline; the authors acknowledge prior work (Athalye et al.) showed LID detectors are evadable by adaptive CW in CV and argue only informally that their shallow fully-connected FNN preserves stronger linear neighbor relationships, without an adaptive-attack experiment to confirm it. (3) Evaluation is binary classification (benign vs malicious) and results are shown only for representative DoS (NSL-KDD) / DDoS (CICIDS2018) attack categories, with other categories claimed 'similar' but not tabulated. (4) LS robustness rests partly on a heuristic Rayleigh-quotient argument rather than a formal guarantee. (5) The framework's benefit depends on the empirical low DL->ML transferability holding, which an attacker aware of the design could target. (6) DeepFool/CW residual accuracy (63.7-72.2%) still leaves substantial evasion room.

datasets_used: NSL-KDD (120,000 training / 25,000 testing samples; 41 features expanded to 128-dim, 35 dynamic + 6 persistent; DoS results shown; FNN model)
CICIDS2018 (1.5M training / 300,000 testing flows; 78 features after cleaning, reshaped to 9x9; DDoS results shown; CNN model)
quantitative_results: NSL-KDD DL-IDS clean accuracy ~94.3%; drops under CW to 63.4% (2% pert), 52.2% (5%), 18.9% (10%), 5.7% (20%) [Table 1]
CICIDS2018 DL-IDS clean accuracy 99.3%; under CW drops to 50.2% (2%), 40.0% (5%), 17.3% (10%), 0% (20%) [Table 1]
NSL-KDD DLL-IDS vs Baseline accuracy at 10% pert: CLEAN 91.8% vs 92.1%, FGSM 90.2% vs 75.7%, BIM 86.2% vs 67.3%, DeepFool 72.2% vs 32.3%, CW 71.7% vs 17.9% [Table 5]
CICIDS2018 DLL-IDS vs Baseline accuracy: CLEAN 98.1% vs 98.5%, FGSM 95.0% vs 32.0%, BIM 88.5% vs 22.5%, DeepFool 71.5% vs 18.5%, CW 63.7% vs 17.1% [Table 6]
LID vs Artifact(DB) AE-detection accuracy on NSL-KDD: CLEAN 96.9% vs 82.1%; FGSM-20% 90.1% vs 66.7%; CW-20% 83.4% vs 89.6% [Table 3]
LID fitting-algorithm accuracy on NSL-KDD: SVM chosen; e.g. CW-attack detection LGR 72.16% / DCT 86.1% / BNB 100% (but BNB FPR too high on clean 5.1%) / SVM 85.5% [Table 2]
ML-model overall accuracy on transferred AEs (NSL-KDD): LS 81.0%, KNN 72.9%, DTC 61.2%, SVM 57.1%, LGR 54.6%, BNB 77.6% [Table 4]
LID detector k parameter set to 10; LID of AEs generally higher than clean examples
baselines_compared: DB (decision-boundary) AE detector from MANDA [ref 26], labeled 'Artifact' -- AE-detection baseline
Candidate ML robustness models: KNN, LGR (logistic regression), BNB (Bernoulli naive Bayes), DTC (decision tree), SVM, LS (Label-Spreading, selected)
Candidate LID-fitting classifiers: LGR, DCT (decision tree), BNB, SVM (selected)
